hallucinations.description: The paper claims the final DAPR classifier uses a two-layer feedforward encoder over a 64-dimensional handcrafted feature vector, but the baseline code identified in `baseline_summary.json` and `best_solution_02ff12e20aca46e09fd807fa573378e3.py` implements a different method: a simpler MLP `DivergenceClassifier` over a 42-dimensional feature vector with no attention, no LayerNorm, no auxiliary divergence-location head, and no 64-dim representation. The paper also states the model 'uses a two-layer feedforward encoder with LayerNorm and dropout (0.2), followed by a classification head' and that it 'experimented with attention and auxiliary divergence prediction'; this corresponds only to later ablation scripts, not the baseline/best solution. Thus the methodology presented as the main method is inconsistent with the actual baseline implementation.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "Our classifier uses a two-layer feedforward encoder with LayerNorm and dropout (0.2), followed by a classification head. We also experimented with attention and auxiliary divergence prediction..." and "We extract a 64-dimensional feature vector... global features (4 dim)... per-step features (60 dim)."
Code (`best_solution_02ff12e20aca46e09fd807fa573378e3.py`): `target_len = 2 + max_len * 2` in `extract_divergence_features`, giving 42 dims, and `class DivergenceClassifier(nn.Module): self.encoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.2), nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.2))` with no attention or divergence head.

hallucinations.description: Several quantitative results reported in the paper do not match the provided code artifacts. The paper's headline results (96.7% test accuracy and cross-dataset accuracies 96.0/98.0/96.6) come from an ablation script with attention/divergence-head architecture, whereas the baseline summary and best solution code report materially lower performance (~90.3% synthetic test, 90.0% CodeXGLUE-style, 93.2% ManyBugs-style). The paper presents these as main experimental results without matching the baseline implementation/code supplied, so the results are not supported by the actual primary experiment.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper Table 1: "Synthetic 96.7  CodeXGLUE 96.0  MBPP 98.0  HumanEval 96.6" and text: "DAPR achieves 96.7% test accuracy..."
Baseline summary (`baseline_summary.json`): best config `lr_0.002_bs_64_hd_128` has `Synthetic Test Accuracy: 0.9033`, `CodeXGLUE Test Accuracy: 0.9`, `ManyBugs Test Accuracy: 0.932`.
`best_solution_02ff12e20aca46e09fd807fa573378e3.py` prints: `Test Accuracy: {experiment_data[best_config]['test_accuracy']:.4f}` for the best config, corresponding to ~0.9033, not 0.9667.

hallucinations.description: The paper reports detailed ablation numbers and figures that are not consistently grounded in the corresponding code outputs. For example, it claims removing per-step trace features drops accuracy from 96.7% to 52.7%, but the provided `no_trace_step_difference_features` ablation code reports 52.67% only for the synthetic test and a much broader collapse pattern; meanwhile the paper's main method itself does not align with the baseline code. More importantly, the paper reports figure-specific confusion counts and per-class accuracies as if they were final validated study outputs, but these are drawn from individual ablation artifacts rather than a coherent reproduced experiment matching the stated method. This makes the reported experimental section effectively fabricated/incoherent relative to the supplied implementation.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "Removing per-step trace features causes accuracy to collapse from 96.7% to 52.7%" and "wrong variable mutations are confused with off-by-one (7 cases) and wrong operator (3 cases)."
Ablation artifact (`ablation_summary.json`, `no_trace_step_difference_features`): `Synthetic Test Classification Accuracy` for ablation is `0.5267`; however the corresponding confusion matrix analysis says `wrong_operator is entirely misclassified as off-by-one (78/78 instances)`, which is not reflected in the paper's main confusion discussion that still centers only on wrong_variable. The paper mixes incompatible result sets from different code paths.

hallucinations.description: At least one citation in the paper appears nonexistent or unsupported. The paper cites 'PPOCoder (Shojaee et al., 2023)' as an execution-guided RL-for-code method. In the references, this is listed as 'Execution-based code generation using deep reinforcement learning' with only an arXiv-style title and authors, but this specific work is not a standard known citation under the name PPOCoder and may be a fabricated mapping from a generic description to a paper title. The paper treats PPOCoder as an established named method, but the provided reference does not substantiate that named system.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "PPOCoder (Shojaee et al., 2023) and RLEF (Gehring et al., 2024) use RL with execution-based rewards..."
References: "P. Shojaee, Aneesh Jain, Sindhu Tipirneni, and Chandan K. Reddy. Execution-based code generation using deep reinforcement learning. ArXiv, abs/2301.13816, 2023."

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issues are methodology/result mismatches: the paper's stated DAPR method and headline metrics do not align with the supplied baseline code and summaries, indicating unsupported or mixed experimental claims. Citation quality is also questionable for at least one named method. Overall severity is moderate to high because the core empirical narrative is not faithfully grounded in the provided implementation.
